title: Days, Months & Telling Time
description: Days of the week, months of the year, and how to say what time it is.
summary_pt_br: Dias, meses, horas. Em inglês, dias e meses SEMPRE começam com letra maiúscula, mesmo no meio de uma frase.

exercises.explanation: The five weekdays. WEDNESDAY is the trickiest — the first 'd' is silent: /ˈwenz.deɪ/.
exercises.hint_pt_br: Wednesday se lê 'wenzdei' — o primeiro 'd' é mudo.

exercises.explanation: The weekend. In the US, the week is often thought of as starting Sunday; in the UK and calendars, Monday.
exercises.hint_pt_br: 'Weekend' = fim de semana. Em inglês americano a semana começa no domingo, no britânico começa na segunda.

exercises.question: Which is spelled correctly?
exercises.explanation: In English, days and months are ALWAYS capitalized — even mid-sentence: 'See you on Monday.' This is different from Portuguese.
exercises.hint_pt_br: REGRA: dias e meses em inglês sempre começam com letra MAIÚSCULA.

exercises.question: Complete: 'I go to the gym ___ Monday.' (on which day)
exercises.explanation: ON + specific day. On Monday, on Tuesday, on my birthday.
exercises.hint_pt_br: ON é a preposição para dias específicos: on Monday, on my birthday.

exercises.question: Complete: 'My birthday is ___ July.' (which month)
exercises.explanation: IN + months, years, seasons, parts of the day. In July, in 2026, in the morning.
exercises.hint_pt_br: IN + mês/ano/estação/parte do dia: in July, in 2026, in the morning.

exercises.explanation: February is often misspelled — 'Feb-RU-ary', with a 'r' that's easy to drop.
exercises.hint_pt_br: February tem um 'r' que muita gente esquece: 'feb-RU-ari'.

exercises.question: It's 3:00 p.m. The most natural way to say the time:
options: Both B and C are common
exercises.explanation: 'It's three o'clock' is traditional; 'It's three' is casual. 'O'clock' is only used with whole hours — never 'three-thirty o'clock'.
exercises.hint_pt_br: 'O'clock' só vai com horas fechadas — nunca com 'e meia' ou 'e quinze'.

exercises.question: How do you say 6:15?
exercises.explanation: 'Six fifteen' (digital style) and 'quarter past six' (traditional) are both natural. For 6:30 → 'six thirty' OR 'half past six'. For 6:45 → 'six forty-five' OR 'quarter to seven'.
exercises.hint_pt_br: 6:15 = 'six fifteen' OU 'quarter past six'. 6:30 = 'six thirty' OU 'half past six'. 6:45 = 'six forty-five' OU 'quarter to seven'.

exercises.question: Complete: 'What ___ is it?' (asking the time)
exercises.explanation: 'What time is it?' — the standard question. Note: NOT 'What hours are?' (Portuguese structure).
exercises.hint_pt_br: 'What time is it?' — não traduza 'que horas são' literalmente.
